title: How to use Wise to pay yourself a 'salary' from your foreign corporation
primary_keyword: wise to pay salary from foreign corp

interlinking_suggestions.title: Separating Business and Personal Finances: A Crucial Step for LLCs
Hiring Your First Subcontractor: Legal and Financial Steps

author.name: Samuel Chen
author.bio: A former product manager at a major fintech company, Samuel has deep expertise in the global payments landscape. He analyzes financial tools and strategies to help freelancers maximize their earnings and minimize fees.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Prioritize building a compliant business structure over choosing a payment tool, as the tool itself offers no legal or tax protection.
Proactively manage your compliance with strong contracts and professional invoices to eliminate client fears of misclassification and establish a B2B partnership.
Assume total responsibility for your tax obligations, including quarterly estimated payments, self-employment taxes, and mandatory FBAR filing for foreign accounts over $10,000.
Establish a formal business entity, like an LLC, to protect your personal assets and solidify your professional standing as an independent contractor rather than a quasi-employee.
summary: Global professionals often mistakenly focus on payment tools like Wise, when the real problem is the lack of a compliant business structure for receiving international payments. The core advice is to build a professional system with strong contracts, compliant invoices, and a formal business entity to proactively manage your own tax and legal obligations. This approach closes critical compliance gaps, mitigates risks for you and your clients, and empowers you to operate as an autonomous business owner with true peace of mind.
ai_overview_answer: Getting paid compliantly as a global professional depends on building a resilient business structure, not on the specific payment tool you use. Services like Wise are efficient payment rails for moving money but do not manage your tax, legal, or reporting responsibilities. The first step is to establish your "compliance armor" through a strong contract that defines you as an independent contractor and prevents employee misclassification. This defense is reinforced by using compliant invoices and providing necessary tax forms like the W-8BEN to clients. As the business owner, you are solely responsible for managing all tax obligations, including estimated taxes, self-employment taxes, and critical filings like the FBAR for US expats. Architecting a formal corporate structure, such as a sole proprietorship or an LLC, solidifies your professional B2B standing. This proactive approach establishes a legitimate direct-contracting relationship, providing a more autonomous alternative to using a restrictive Employer of Record (EOR).
disclaimer: The information provided in this article is for informational purposes only and does not constitute legal or financial advice. You should consult with a qualified professional for advice tailored to your individual situation.

author.name: Samuel Chen
author.description: A former product manager at a major fintech company, Samuel has deep expertise in the global payments landscape. He analyzes financial tools and strategies to help freelancers maximize their earnings and minimize fees.

step.name: Tier 1: Choosing Your Payment Rail
step.text: Once you reframe the engagement around your professional process, the mechanics of moving money become a simple, tactical choice. The tool that carries money from your client’s bank to yours is a payment rail, and agonizing over which one to use is the least important decision you will make. Your evaluation criteria should be ruthlessly simple: Speed, Cost, and Functionality. Platforms like Wise and Revolut are dominant players because they excel at these core functions. The critical takeaway is this: your payment rail offers zero protection. These platforms are the pipes, not the fortress.

step.name: Tier 2: Building Your 'Compliance Armor'
step.text: Moving money is easy; protecting it—and the business that earned it—is where your focus must shift. This isn't about finding loopholes; it's about establishing an unimpeachable framework that insulates you from risk. This includes having a bulletproof Master Services Agreement (MSA) as your first line of defense against misclassification, creating compliant invoices to ensure faster payment, providing essential forms like the W-8BEN for US clients, and establishing a personal system for managing your own tax and reporting obligations, including estimated taxes, self-employment tax, and FBAR filing.

step.name: Tier 3: Architecting Your Corporate Structure
step.text: To truly operate with authority, you must elevate your thinking from managing compliance tasks to architecting a professional business structure. This begins with the mindset shift that you are not receiving a 'salary' but are a business collecting on an invoice. Choose a business structure like a Sole Proprietorship or an LLC, with the latter offering crucial liability protection. This formal structure allows you to reframe the conversation with clients who suggest an Employer of Record (EOR), positioning direct contracting as the definitive path to empowerment and autonomy.

mainEntity.name: Does Wise handle my taxes for international payments?

acceptedAnswer.text: No. Wise is a financial institution that provides a payment rail; it is not a tax or payroll service. As the business owner, you are entirely responsible for reporting all income and fulfilling your tax obligations.

mainEntity.name: What are my main tax responsibilities as a US contractor paid by a foreign company?

acceptedAnswer.text: Your primary duties include paying estimated quarterly taxes to the IRS, paying the full 15.3% self-employment tax (covering Social Security and Medicare), and filing an FBAR if the combined balance of your foreign accounts exceeds $10,000 at any point during the year.

mainEntity.name: Is an EOR the only way to get paid compliantly from a foreign company?

acceptedAnswer.text: Absolutely not. For an independent professional, direct contracting is often the superior path. An Employer of Record (EOR) is one tool, but it positions you as a quasi-employee. The more empowering alternative is to establish a direct Business-to-Business (B2B) relationship through strong contracts, professional invoicing, and diligent self-management of your compliance.

mainEntity.name: How do I receive payments from a foreign client without using an EOR?

acceptedAnswer.text:
You operate as a legitimate, independent business. This structure is built on four pillars:
1. A robust Master Services Agreement defining you as an independent contractor.
2. Compliant invoices containing all legally required information.
3. A reliable payment rail, like Wise, to move funds from their business to yours.
4. Proactive management of your own tax and reporting obligations.

mainEntity.name: What is an FBAR and why is it so important for US expats?

acceptedAnswer.text: The FBAR (Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts) is an annual report filed with the U.S. Treasury's Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN). It is required if the aggregate value of your foreign financial accounts exceeds $10,000 at any time during the year. The penalties for failure to file are severe, starting at over $10,000 for non-willful violations, making compliance a non-negotiable cornerstone of your financial health.

mainEntity.name: Why do I need to give my US-based client a W-8BEN form?

acceptedAnswer.text: The Form W-8BEN certifies to your US client that you are not a U.S. person for tax purposes. Its function is to instruct them not to withhold the default 30% tax from your payments. It is a standard professional document that ensures proper tax treatment for a foreign independent contractor.

body.content: The Real Question Isn't "How to Use Wise," It's "How to Get Paid Compliantly"

For the independent global professional, the mechanics of receiving international payments can feel like a high-stakes puzzle. We obsess over the tools—comparing fees, transfer times, and currency options—believing the right app will solve the problem. But this focus is a distraction from the real challenge.

The most important question isn't about technology; it's about structure. Using a tool like Wise is straightforward. Building a resilient, compliant system to get paid is what provides true peace of mind. This begins with understanding what your tools can—and absolutely cannot—do for your "Business-of-One."

Wise is an exceptional payment rail, not a compliance solution. Think of it as the world-class plumbing that moves your money. As a registered Money Services Business (MSB), its job is to transmit funds from point A to point B with incredible efficiency. A Wise Business account is a fantastic instrument for this tactical task. But its mandate ends the moment the money arrives.

This creates a critical "Compliance Gap"—the space where professional anxiety lives. Relying on your payment rail for anything more than the transfer itself is a high-risk strategy. This gap includes core business responsibilities that Wise, by design, does not and cannot manage:

items: **Calculating Tax Liability:** It will not calculate your federal, state, or the all-important self-employment taxes. As the business owner, that responsibility is entirely yours.
**Verifying Legal Status:** The tool cannot adjudicate whether you are a true independent contractor or a misclassified employee in the eyes of the law.

**Ensuring Invoice Legitimacy:** It does not verify that your invoice contains the legally required information for a cross-border B2B transaction, such as a client's VAT ID and "reverse-charge" notation for EU clients.
**Managing FBAR Reporting:** For U.S. expats, this is a catastrophic risk. Wise will not track your account balances or file a Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts (FBAR) on your behalf.

body.content: Your client is just as concerned with compliance, primarily fearing employee misclassification. Accidentally treating a contractor like an employee can expose them to severe penalties. This is why many default to using a restrictive Employer of Record (EOR)—it shields them from that specific risk.

Your strategic goal is to proactively address their fears by presenting a more professional alternative: a legitimate, well-documented B2B engagement that you control. This transforms the conversation from their risk mitigation to your professional process, positioning you not as a freelancer seeking payment, but as a strategic partner providing services.
Tier 1: Choosing Your Payment Rail (The Least Important Decision You'll Make)

Once you reframe the engagement around your professional process, the mechanics of moving money become a simple, tactical choice. This is where many professionals get stuck, endlessly comparing features on what is essentially a commodity service. The tool that carries money from your client’s bank to yours is a payment rail, and agonizing over which one to use is the least important decision you will make.
Your evaluation criteria should be ruthlessly simple:

items: **Speed:** How quickly do funds settle in your account?
**Cost:** Does the service provide access to the mid-market exchange rate, ensuring you aren't losing money to hidden markups?
**Functionality:** Can you easily hold multi-currency balances to manage funds from different clients?

body.content: Platforms like Wise and Revolut are dominant players because they excel at these core functions. They are interchangeable instruments, and choosing between them is less a strategic decision and more a matter of preference—like picking between two excellent hammer brands to build a house.

rows: Broader financial toolkit with cards and expense tools.
Mid-market rate with a clear, upfront fee.
Mid-market rate, but markups can apply on weekends.
Holds 40+ currencies with local details for 9+.

body.content: The critical takeaway is this: **your payment rail offers zero protection.** These platforms are the pipes, not the fortress. Their mandate is to move money, not to ensure your business is legally sound, that you've calculated your taxes correctly, or that your contract shields you from misclassification. The real work begins *after* the money has been moved.
Tier 2: Building Your "Compliance Armor" (The Layer That Protects Your Business)

Moving money is easy; protecting it—and the business that earned it—is where your focus must shift. If the payment rail is the foundation, your compliance armor is the fortress you build upon it. This isn't about finding loopholes; it's about establishing an unimpeachable framework that insulates you from risk.

items: **The Bulletproof Contract: Your First Line of Defense.** Before any work begins, your Master Services Agreement (MSA) must do the heavy lifting. This document is your primary defense against misclassification. It must unambiguously define your relationship as an independent contractor by including clauses that detail your control over *how* you perform your work, your responsibility for your own tools, and your right to delegate tasks. These are the clear boundaries that separate a business from a disguised employee.

**The Compliant Invoice: The Key to Faster Payment.** An invoice is more than a request for payment; it's a professional document that must pass through a corporate finance department. To eliminate friction, always include your client’s full, registered business name and address, their VAT ID (especially in the EU), and any legally required text for cross-border services (e.g., "VAT Reverse-Charged"). A clean, compliant invoice signals professionalism, removes delays, and reinforces your standing.

**The Essential Forms: Demystifying the W-8BEN.** When working with US clients, providing a correctly filled W-8BEN form is a non-negotiable step. This form is simply you, a non-US person, certifying your foreign status. Its purpose is to instruct your client *not* to withhold the default 30% US income tax from your payments. It protects both parties and demonstrates you are managing your own tax affairs responsibly.

**Your Personal Tax & Reporting System.** As the founder and CFO of your enterprise, the ultimate responsibility for tax and reporting rests on your shoulders. For US citizens abroad, this system is built on several non-negotiables:
**Estimated Taxes:** You must proactively set aside a percentage of every payment to cover your federal and, if applicable, state tax liabilities, paying the IRS quarterly.
**Self-Employment Tax:** You are responsible for both the employee and employer portions of Social Security and Medicare taxes, which amounts to a 15.3% tax on your net self-employment income.

**FBAR Filing:** This is the highest-stakes compliance task for Americans abroad. If the combined total of your foreign financial accounts exceeds $10,000 at any point during the year, you are legally required to file a Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts (FBAR). As Seth Hertz, Director at Expat US Tax, bluntly states, "For US citizens living abroad, understanding the FBAR is not optional – it's a critical annual requirement with serious consequences for non-compliance."

body.content: Tier 3: Architecting Your Corporate Structure (The Engine of Your Autonomy)

With your compliance armor in place, you’ve mitigated immediate risks. To truly operate with authority, you must elevate your thinking from managing compliance tasks to architecting a professional business structure. This is the engine of your autonomy.

items: **The Foundational Mindset Shift: You Are Not Receiving a "Salary."** This is the most powerful conceptual leap you must make. Employees receive salaries; independent businesses submit invoices for services rendered. This isn't just semantics; it’s a legal and strategic distinction. The IRS defines an independent contractor as someone whose client has the right to control only the *result* of the work, not *how* it is done. By sending a professional invoice from your business to theirs, you reinforce this B2B boundary. This reframes the problem entirely: you are a business owner collecting on an invoice, not an employee figuring out how to get paid.

**Sole Proprietor vs. LLC: Choosing Your Business Structure.** Your business structure is the vessel that contains your professional activities. The two most common choices present a clear trade-off between simplicity and protection.

rows: None. You and the business are legally the same. Your personal assets are at risk.
Provides a legal shield between your personal and business finances.
Can be viewed as less formal by larger corporate clients.
Seen as a more professional, stable, and trustworthy entity.
Simple and inexpensive to start with minimal ongoing paperwork.
Requires state registration, filing fees, and more formal compliance.

body.content: For a global professional managing significant contracts, forming an LLC is a strategic investment in risk reduction. It creates a formal legal separation that protects your personal life from your business life—a foundational element of long-term resilience.

items: **Reframing the EOR: An Option, Not the Only Option.** You will encounter clients who suggest using an Employer of Record (EOR), a third party that legally hires and pays a worker on their behalf. For some, this offers a path of least resistance. However, for the professional who values autonomy, it's often a step backward. An EOR can blur the lines you’ve worked to establish, treating you more like a quasi-employee than a business owner. While it mitigates risk for the client, it does so by inserting a permanent intermediary. For the true "Business-of-One," direct contracting isn't just an alternative; it is the definitive path to empowerment.

body.content: Frequently Asked Questions
Navigating the architecture of your global business will naturally surface questions. Here are direct answers to the most common queries we see.

items:
**Does Wise handle my taxes for international payments?**
No. Wise is a financial institution that provides a payment rail; it is not a tax or payroll service. As the business owner, you are entirely responsible for reporting all income and fulfilling your tax obligations.

**What are my main tax responsibilities as a US contractor paid by a foreign company?**
Your primary duties include paying estimated quarterly taxes to the IRS, paying the full 15.3% self-employment tax (covering Social Security and Medicare), and filing an FBAR if the combined balance of your foreign accounts exceeds $10,000 at any point during the year.

**Is an EOR the only way to get paid compliantly from a foreign company?**
Absolutely not. For an independent professional, direct contracting is often the superior path. An Employer of Record (EOR) is one tool, but it positions you as a quasi-employee. The more empowering alternative is to establish a direct Business-to-Business (B2B) relationship through strong contracts, professional invoicing, and diligent self-management of your compliance.

**How do I receive payments from a foreign client without using an EOR?**
You operate as a legitimate, independent business. This structure is built on four pillars:
1.  A robust **Master Services Agreement** defining you as an independent contractor.
2.  Compliant **invoices** containing all legally required information.
3.  A reliable **payment rail**, like Wise, to move funds from their business to yours.
4.  Proactive **management of your own tax and reporting obligations**.

**What is an FBAR and why is it so important for US expats?**
The FBAR (Report of Foreign Bank and Financial Accounts) is an annual report filed with the U.S. Treasury's Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN). It is required if the aggregate value of your foreign financial accounts exceeds $10,000 at any time during the year. The penalties for failure to file are severe, starting at over $10,000 for non-willful violations, making compliance a non-negotiable cornerstone of your financial health.

**Why do I need to give my US-based client a W-8BEN form?**
The Form W-8BEN certifies to your US client that you are not a U.S. person for tax purposes. Its function is to instruct them *not* to withhold the default 30% tax from your payments. It is a standard professional document that ensures proper tax treatment for a foreign independent contractor.

body.content: Conclusion: You're Not Just a Freelancer; You're the CEO

Getting paid internationally has almost nothing to do with the app you use. It has everything to do with the system you build. The debate over payment tools is a distraction from the real work: to stop thinking like a passive payee and start operating like the CEO of your own global enterprise.

Freelancers trade time for money; CEOs build systems that generate revenue and protect the business. This is the fundamental mindset shift that unlocks true autonomy. Your operational structure rests on the three-tier framework we've outlined—a blueprint for moving from reactive anxiety to proactive control.

items: **Tier 1: The Payment Rail.** This is the tactical plumbing. Choose an efficient, low-cost option and move on. The rail itself offers zero legal or tax protection.

**Tier 2: The Compliance Armor.** This is your active defense against risk, built from strong contracts, bulletproof invoices, and a diligent system for managing your taxes and reporting. This armor separates amateurs from professionals.

**Tier 3: The Corporate Structure.** This is the engine of your independence. Operating as a legitimate business entity—whether a sole proprietorship or, for greater protection, an LLC—is the ultimate source of your control. This B2B positioning makes a restrictive EOR unnecessary and allows you to dictate the terms of your engagement.

body.content: Architecting your business this way is the definitive answer to the compliance question. It transforms a complex challenge into a straightforward process that you own from end to end. This is how you build a resilient, profitable, and truly independent global career.
